rules.9x9: Numbers placed along the marked line and circles must no repeat. The same set of 8 numbers is used along the circles.
The range of digits is now 0-9. Some cells have been split with a diagonal, and two digits go into those cells.

Place a digit from 0-8 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and 3x3 box contains each digit exactly once. Each digit placed in a cell with a circle must be the sum of the digits placed in the cells that the adjoining arrow passes through. Digits may repeat on arrows.

Each number from 1-8 represents the height of the building. The clues outside the grid indicate the number of buildings visible from the corresponding direction. A taller building will hide any shorter buildings behind it. 0 does not represent a building, and does not count towards the number of buildings in a corresponding direction.

The three numbers placed along marked lines must result in 24 after applying two of the arithmetic operations (+, x, -, /). The order of these numbers along the line does not matter. E.g. 395 can be combined as 24 = (3 x 5) + 9.

Place a digit from 1-8 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and outlined regions contains each digit exactly once. This puzzle has 12 lines (instead of rows and columns) and 6 regions. The lines are not straight. The way to identify a line is by taking a cell at the edge of the grid and to follow a path by finding the next cell that touches the opposite side of the cell. You repeat this process until you reach the other edge.
In each outlined region, all shaded cells must contain digits of the same parity.

instructions: Use the Cell tool to shade every constrained cell. Shaded cells are grouped by their Sudoku box.

rules.9x9: Numbers formed by the digits on the gray lines are "almost palindromes", i.e. they can be read equally in both directions except for exactly one couple of cells that do not match on each line.
Along each line, digits alternate between being larger and smaller than their immediate neighbours. Digits do not repeat on a line.

instructions: Line · diagonal · grey
Draw every alternating stripe as a grey diagonal-style line.

rules.9x9: Each main diagonal contains exactly three digits.

instructions: No mark is required. Select this button to inspect or activate the rule.

rules.9x9: No cell that is a knight-step away can contain the same digit. A knight's move is 2 in a line and 1 to the side, as in chess.

instructions: This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

rules.9x9: Adjacent cells marked with X cannot contain digits which are consecutive to each other. Not all such adjacent cells are marked. In other words, adjacent cells with no marking may or may not contain consecutive digits.
No digit can repeat in any of the marked diagonals.
Each digit between adjacent cells is the sum or difference or product or ratio of digits in those two cells.
The digit in each circled cell is the sum of digits along the path of its arrow. Digits can repeat within an arrow shape.

instructions: Drag from the circle cell through the shaft cells to create an arrow.

rules.9x9: There are circles and arrows in the grid. For a digit N in a circle, the digit N+1 is at the end of the attached arrow. The digits along the arrow (not including the circle) must form a sequence of consecutive digits. (The digit in the circle can appear along the arrow itself.)
Clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the first set of all ascending digits, starting from the first cell from the corresponding side. Sets may consist of only one digit.

If the digit in a cell is the average of its two horizontal neighbours then the cell is marked with a horizontal bar. If the digit in a cell is the average of its two vertical neighbours then the cell is marked with a vertical bar. All possible bars are marked.
The number placed in a cell with a circle must be the arithmetic mean of the digits along the path of each arrow. Numbers may repeat on an arrow.
Each 2x2 area with two odd digits and two even digits forming a checkerboard pattern is marked with a battenburg symbol (or circle). All such 2x2 areas are marked.

instructions: Click a four-cell corner to add or remove a Battenburg mark; use its Negative toggle to constrain unmarked corners.

rules.9x9: Each ship outside the grid must appear in the grid such that ships do not touch each other, even diagonally. Rotation and reflection are allowed but the orientation of digits in the ships remain the same. Ship segments without a digit can contain any digit. Each number outside the grid is the number of ship segments in the corresponding row or column. Cells with given digits or a water symbol are not part of any ship.
Place the given set of battleships into the grid; ships cannot touch even diagonally. The clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the digits occupied by ships in the corresponding row or column.
Numbers outside the grid indicate the sum of all digits before the digit 1 in that row/column or after the digit 9 in that row/column, going from left to right and from top to bottom.
The clues outside the grid show the sum of the digits that appear before 9 in the corresponding row or column.
Every interior digit on a line lies strictly between the digits in the circles at the two ends. Digits may repeat on a line.

instructions: Line · normal
Shape · large white circle
Draw the line and place a white circle on each endpoint.

rules.9x9: Each of the grey twisty bands must contain one of the given 7-digit numbers. The numbers can be entered in either of two directions.

Clues outside the grid indicate the sums of groups in the given order. Clues on top refer to groups of big digits (5-9). These groups are separated by at least one small digit (1-4). Clues on the left refer to groups of small digits, that are separated by at least one big digit.
Each arrow(s) in a cell points to the largest digit among the digits in its adjacent cells.
Each inequality sign between boxes compares the sums of the corresponding rows or columns on its two sides.

instructions: Number · edge
Place <, ^, >, or v between the two block slices being compared.

rules.9x9: Each number outside the grid is the sum of the first X cells along the indicated diagonal, starting from that cell, where X is the first digit placed in that direction. If the diagonal hits the edge, it bounces at a 90 degree angle. Digits may be repeated in the sums.

instructions: Number · normal
Shape · diagonal arrow
Place the total and its arrow on the same outside cell.

rules.9x9: If two neighbouring cells are connected with a grey line, they contain consecutive digits. All possible sequences with length of at least 3 are marked inside each 3x3 box.
The dots given inside some cells form part (or all) of the Braille representation of the digit.
Each number outside the grid indicates the first cell where the sum of digits in the corresponding direction is greater than 21.

instructions: Number · small
Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.9x9: Place a number from 1-8 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked region each number exactly once. Note that cells containing capsules span three columns.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Symbols around the grid describe in correct order all increasing and decreasing sequences of length 3 or more that exist in the corresponding row/column. Sharp end of each symbol points where the small numbers are, open end is where the big numbers are. Number inside a symbol gives the length of a sequence.

Two numbers are white and black chess Kings. As in real chess, Kings can not touch other Kings, not even diagonally, even if they are not in opposite color. It is a part of solving to find which numbers are chess Kings.
Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined region.

instructions: Click a cell, then type a digit. Use Tab to cycle through available input tools.

rules.9x9: Each 1x4 marked area represents a digital clock that displays a valid time from left-to-right in 24-hour (HH:MM) format.

instructions: Cage
Draw a cage around each horizontal group of four clock digits; other cage shapes are ignored.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 0-8 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked 3x3 box contains each digit exactly once. Some digital clocks are shown. When the puzzle is completed, all clocks should display a valid time in 24-hour format (HH:MM); i.e. a clock cannot display 43:65.

Each 2x2 area with digits increasing in clockwise direction is marked with a white circle at the intersection. Each 2x2 area with digits increasing in anti-clockwise direction is marked with a black circle at the intersection. All such circles are marked.
Digits in the same corresponding cell in each shaded figure must be identical.

instructions: Draw cages around matching shapes. Every cage must have at least one other cage with exactly the same shape.

rules.9x9: Each shape in the grid appears four times. Two shapes are clones, and digits in the same place in each shape must be identical. Other two shapes are renban groups, and they must contain a set of distinct, consecutive digits in any order. Cloned shapes may or may not be renban groups at the same time, and digits in it may repeat. Identifying which shapes are clones and which are renban groups is part of the solving.

Each shape is cloned, including the digits it contains, without rotating or mirroring. The position of the digits inside each shape is fixed - the same digits have to be placed at the same position inside the identical shapes. The digit may appear more than once in a shape.
Every marked strand has exactly the same sequence of digits. A digit may repeat on a strand.

instructions: Line · diagonal
Draw the equal strands as diagonal grey lines of the same length.

rules.9x9: Some digits have been substituted by letters. All instances of the same letter must be substituted by the same digit and different letters must be substituted by different digits.

instructions: Number · corner
Enter the code letter at the upper-left corner of its cell.

rules.9x9: Two cages with a dashed border are marked by the same letter if they contain the same set of digits. For each cage marked with the same letter digits in the same place must be identical. Digits may repeat in a cage.
Two-cell cages marked with the same letter contain the same pair of digits in either order.

instructions: Cage
Number · erer
Cage each pair and give matching pairs the same code.

rules.9x9: The clues with an arrow outside the grid indicate the number of even digits in the indicated direction. However, some of these clues are replaced by letters. Different letters mean different numbers. Lettered clues can be equal to any of the given numbered clues.
Adjacent cells marked with a bar contain consecutive digits. All possible bars are marked.
Digits in the grey areas must form a chain of consecutive digits from 1 to 9 by moving horizontally or vertically from one cell to the next.
Digits in the same corresponding cell in each shaded figure must be consecutive.

If a circle is given between two adjacent cells, then the digits in those cells must be consecutive. Not all circles are given; adjacent cells without a circle may contain either consecutive numbers or non-consecutive numbers.

Each white circle at the intersection of four cells indicates that there is exactly one consecutive pair of digits in those four cells. Each black circle at the intersection of four cells indicates that there are at least two consecutive pairs of digits in those four cells. Different pairs can use the same digit. Not all possible circles are marked.
Each digit assigned to C is in a corner cell of the 3x3 outlined region pointed by its arrow. Each digit assigned to E is in an edge cell of the 3x3 outlined region pointed by its arrow.
Follow classic sudoku rules. Number in a circle tells how many distinct digits you can find along the corresponding arrow.
Number in a circle tells the count of odd digits in up to 10 cells touching the pair of cells with the circle.
Follow classic sudoku rules. Number in a circle tells you how many odd digits you can find along the corresponding arrow.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Number in a cell with a circle tells you how many distinct digits you can find in up to 8 neighbouring cells (sharing edge or corner). Number in a cell with a cross tells you how many distinct digits you can find in up to 4 diagonally neighbouring cells (sharing a corner). All possible circles and crosses are drawn.
Digits along each line are monotonically increasing or decreasing.

instructions: Special · no-bulb thermo
Draw each monotonic line with the no-bulb thermo tool.

rules.9x9: At every marked four-cell intersection, the two diagonal pairs have equal sums. All such intersections are marked.

instructions: Shape · cross
Place a cross at each four-cell intersection.

rules.9x9: Place all the listed number sequences into shaded strips. Number sequences can be entered in any of four directions.

Each number outside the grid must appear on the diagonal indicated by its corresponding arrow. If a number is given multiple times for a single arrow, that number must appear at least as many times as it is specified on that diagonal.

instructions: Number · normal
Shape · diagonal arrow
Place the digits and its arrow on the same outside cell.

rules.9x9: There are some white and grey arrows in the grid. The digit placed in the cell with white arrow must not repeat in the direction of the arrow. The digit placed in the cell with grey arrow is repeated at least once in the direction of the arrow.

instructions: Shape · diagonal arrow
White arrows must not repeat in the direction of the arrow. Grey arrows must repeat at least once.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. No digit can repeat in any outlined region.

instructions: Enter a region number in every cell. Equal numbers form an outlined region; Deficit regions may contain fewer than the grid-size number of cells.

rules.9x9: Kropki dots are placed in the grid. Kropki rules are applied only for a pair of cells where the centers of cells are connected (orthogonally or diagonally) with a grey line.
For each pair of digits (X and Y) outside the grid, either X is in the Y-th position in the corresponding direction or Y is in the X-th position in the corresponding direction or both.

instructions: Number · normal
Enter a two-digit XY clue in the outside margin.

rules.9x9: A diagonal arrow in a cell containing N points toward another N on that diagonal sightline. All possible arrows are drawn.

instructions: Shape · eight-direction arrow
Place only the diagonal arrows that detect the origin digit.

rules.9x9: The determinants of 2x2 matrices are given in the center of the four cells. The determinant of a 2x2 matrix is a a x d - b x c, where a is the top left, b is the top right, c is bottom left and d is the bottom right element.

instructions: Number · circled corner
Enter the determinant at the four-cell intersection.

rules.9x9: Each main diagonal contains each digit from 1 to 9.

instructions: No mark is required. Select this button to inspect or activate the rule.

rules.9x9: One marked diagonal must contains digits from 1-9 exactly once and the other contains exactly 3 different digits.

Follow classic sudoku rules. There are exactly 13 pairs of cells in the grid (15 in example) satisfying the following conditions: one cell is grey, one cell is white, both contain the same digit, which is equal to the diagonal distance between the cells. There is no such pair with both cells white or both cells grey. Each digit from 1 to 8 appears at least once in a grey cell. Each cell / digit belongs to at most one pair.
All pairs of diagonally adjacent cells that contain digits summing to nine are marked with a line; diagonally adjacent cells without a line cannot sum to nine.

instructions: Shape · paired bars
Toggle the left and right diagonal bars at each intersection.

rules.9x9: All pairs of diagonally adjacent cells that contain digits summing to ten are marked with a line; diagonally adjacent cells without a line cannot sum to ten.

instructions: Shape · paired bars
Toggle the left and right diagonal bars at each intersection.

rules.9x9: Diagonally touching cells that contain consecutive digits are marked by a bar. All possible bars are marked.

instructions: Shape · paired bars
Toggle the left and right diagonal bars at each intersection.

rules.9x9: Digits in diagonally touching cells must not be consecutive.
Each digit between adjacent cells is the difference of digits in those two cells.

Each digit in a grey cell must have at least one orthogonally adjacent digit (in eather a grey or a white cell) that is exactly 2 greater or smaller. There may also be digits in white cells which have orthohonally adjacent digits that are exactly 2 greater or smaller.

instructions: Surface
Shade each cell subject to the constraint.

rules.9x9: The small number in a two-cell cage indicates how many different digits surround the cage. Up to 10 cells touching the cage by a side or by a corner should be inspected.

For each grey line, there must be at least one way of removing a digit so that the remaining digits on the line form a palindrome. It is permissible that the digits on the grey line also form a palindrom before the removal step.
No digit can appear in the same cell position in different 3x3 outlined regiones.
Any two cells that share a border between two different 3x3 boxes must contain digits of opposite parity.

instructions: This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

rules.9x9: Nine grey cells are uniquely paired to nine occurances of digit 5, the distance (vertical, horizontal or diagonal) from grey cell to digit 5 is given in the grey cell. Digit 5 cannot be placed in a grey cell.

Outside clues are of the form 'X-Y:Z', which indicates that digit X comes before digit Y (from left-to-right for rows, or top-to-bottom for columns) and the distance between them is exactly Z cells (where Z=1 means X and Y are next to each other).

instructions: Number · normal
Click an outside clue cell and type its X-Y:Z clue (e.g. 2-5:3).
Click an outside clue cell and type its X-Y:Z clue (e.g. 2-5:4).

rules.9x9: Inside each 3x3 box all sums created by three horizontally or vertically adjacent digits must be divisible by 3.

Some clues are marked at the edges of adjacent pairs of cells. These clues must be divisors of the 2 digit numbers formed by the numbers placed in these cells, when read left-to-right or top-to-bottom.

instructions: Number · circled edge
Enter the divisor between the two adjacent cells.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1-4 in each empty cell so that each digit appears exactly twice in each row, column, and outlined region. Identical digits cannot share an edge.

Adjacent cells contianing numbers whose difference is 2 are marked with a white rhombus. Adjacent cells containing numbers whose ratio is 4 are marked with a black rhombus. Adjacent cells with no marking must not contain numbers either whose difference is 2 or whose ratio is 4.
Clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the digit(s) placed between the digits 1, 5 and 9 in the respective row or column in the correct order. The order of the digits 1,5 and 9 is not given.
Each 9 in the grid sees exactly 8 other cells in the 4 vertical and horizontal directions before a wall is seen. Numbers placed in each of these sets of 8 cells must not repeat.

Place a digit from the given set into each of the empty cells so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined region. The remaining cells are blank. Each digit outside the grid is the first digit seen from the corresponding direction.
Each number outside the grid is the difference of digits in the first and the last cell in the corresponding direction.
A circled digit between two cells appears in one of those two cells.

instructions: Number · circled edge
Enter the value that must occur in either adjacent cell.

rules.9x9: Each digit in a cell with an arrow does not appear in any of the cells pointed by its arrow.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Several cells are marked with a circle. In all four directions, the maximal possible length of a path starting from the circle is given until the sum of the digits in the cells along the path is higher than the value of the emitter.
Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits subtract to the same absolute difference. All possible crosses are marked.

instructions: Shape · black cross (option 4)

rules.9x9: Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits multiply to the same product. All possible crosses are marked.

instructions: Shape · black cross (option 4)

rules.9x9: Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits divide to the same ratios. All possible crosses are marked.

instructions: Shape · black cross (option 4)

rules.9x9: The line is divided by the borders of the 3x3 boxes. The digits on a line segment within any given 3x3 box must sum to the same total as the digits on every other segment of that same line.

instructions: Line · diagonal
Draw the lines of the same length.
Draw lines through cells.

rules.9x9: Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits add to the same sum. All possible crosses are marked.

instructions: Shape · black cross (option 4)

rules.9x9: Each of the shaded cells has an escape path to the edge of the grid. An escape path consists of a series of adjacent cells containing a descending consecutive sequence of digits. All escape paths begin with the digit in the shaded cell and end at a cell containing a 1 at the edge of the grid. Escape paths starting in different shaded cells never share any cell. It is possible that a shaded cell can have more than one escape path.
There must be at least one continuous path of cells containing only even digits that travels orthogonally from the top-left corner (r1c1) to the bottom-right corner (r9c9).

instructions: No placed input
The constraint is generated from grid geometry.
This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

rules.9x9: Each digit outside the grid indicates that the digit is sandwiched between two even digits in its adjacent cells in the corresponding direction. All such digits are given.

instructions: Number · normal
Enter every sandwiched digit in one of the three top or left margin layers; leave all three blank to require no sandwiched digit.

rules.9x9: Adjacent cells marked by a circle contain digits whose sum is even. All possible circles are not marked.

instructions: Shape · small white circle
Place one white circle on the shared edge of each marked pair.

rules.9x9: Each digit at the intersection of cells must not be present in any of those cells.
The rows and columns continue over an empty space.

The clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the first X digits placed in the corresponding direction, where X is equal to the first digit placed in that direction. There are different letters and mathematical expressions (consisting of only +, -, x and /) inside and outside the grid. Each letter represents a positive integer. Different letters may take on the same value.
Each marked 10-cell string must contain all digits from 1 to 9. Digits at both ends of each string must be the same.
Each marked region must contain each digit from 1-9 exactly twice.

instructions: Surface
Shade each extra large region as one orthogonally connected group of cells.

rules.9x9: Each marked region contains each digit from 1 to 9.

instructions: Surface
Shade each extra region as one orthogonally connected group of cells.

rules.9x9: The grey cells will not contain numbers.
For any two adjacent digits on the line, one digit must be a factor or a multiple of the other.

instructions: Line · diagonal
Draw the lines of the same length.
Draw lines through cells.

rules.9x9: A white dot between two adjacent cells indicates that the digits in those cells must either be consecutive (differ by 1) or have a 1:2 ratio. All possible white dots are given: cells without a dot cannot be consecutive or have a 1:2 ratio.

instructions: Shape · small white circle
Place white dots between adjacent cells.
Place white dots between adjacent cells. Negative constraint can be toggled using the Negative Kropki button.

rules.9x9: Place the given set of figures into the grid following grid lines. Figures can be rotated, but not reflected. Figures cannot touch each other even in a point. Clues show the sum of neighboring digits which are inside the figures.
Dotted areas with the same shape must contain the same set of digits (but not necessarily in the same order). The digit may be repeated in area.

The digit in each shaded cell is greater than the digits in the first cell of the corresponding row (leftmost cell) and the first cell of the corresponding column (topmost cell). Not all possible cells are shaded.

Clues outside the grid indicate the first digit of a specific parity (odd or even) encountered when looking into the grid from that direction. Any number of opposite-parity digits may appear before the first seen digit.

instructions: Number · normal
Enter the parity clue digit in the margin.
Click an outside cell and type the digit.

rules.9x9: Two cells separated by a diamond must contain digits that have a sum of 5 or a difference of 5. All possible diamonds are given: cells without a diamond cannot have a sum or difference of 5.

instructions: Shape · diamond
Place diamond marks between adjacent cells.

rules.9x9: Fill in the grid with digits 1 to 9 so that every row, column and outlined region contains nine different digits. There are two grids to be solved on two sides of the paper. There are 12 extra squares along the grid edge. You have to bend each of those extra squares (independently) to the one or the other side to form two classic sudokus of the standard size. You should solve them after that.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Grey circles represent 14 football players divided into two teams. Players of each team are marked with numbers from 1 to 7. One team plays with a ball number 8, the other with a ball number 9. Player number 1 has a ball number next to him in a direction of a horizontal, vertical or diagonal pass to the player number 2. And so on up to the pass between players 6 and 7 of each team. No other player stands in the way of every pass.
If a shaded cell and an unshaded cell are adjacent then the digit in the shaded cell is larger.

Follow classic sudoku rules. There are two independent groups of eight cells in the grid, marked with a grey gg. Exactly four different digits can be found in each of the groups, each of them exactly twice.
Numbers outside the grid are equal to the sum of the first 3 digits appearing in the corresponding row or column.
Each number outside the grid is the sum of the first 3 cells along the indicated diagonal.

instructions: Number · normal
Shape · eight-direction arrow
Place diagonal arrow outside and enter its sum.
Place diagonal arrow symbols outside the grid, and enter the sum digits.

rules.9x9: A circle marked at the intersection of a 2×2 block of cells indicates that numbers placed in these cells are either all odd or all even. A square marked at the intersection of a 2×2 block of cells indicates that exactly two numbers placed in these cells are odd, and exactly two numbers are even.

Each row and column forms two n-digit numbers, where n is the grid size, read in opposite directions. Each number outside the grid indicates the rank of the corresponding n-digit number, starting from the smallest.

Place a number from 1 to X (integers only) into each cell so that each number appears exactly once in each row and column. (X is the number of cells in each row.) Some inequality relations are given between adjacent cells; they must apply to the numbers in those cells. Numbers may not repeat in any of the outlined regions.

Exactly one circle should be added on each grey line. One or two arrows should be drawn at all loose ends of each grey line then. Finally solve standard arrows sudoku. A number placed in a cell with a circle must be the sum of the numbers placed in cells the adjoining arrow passes through. Numbers may repeat on arrows.
Adjacent digits along the line must differ by at least 5.

instructions: Line · diagonal
Draw the lines of the same length.
Draw lines through cells.

rules.9x9: Each digit between adjacent cells is the larger of the digits in those two cells.
Each number between two neighbouring cells is either the greater of the digits or the difference between the digits in those two cells.
Each number between two neighbouring cells is either the greater of the digits or the sum of the digits in those two cells.

Follow classic sudoku rules. There are several cages in the grid with a dashed-line outline. Sum of all digits in every cage is given. Same digit cannot be repeated in one cage. All digits should follow given inequality signs.
All equality and inequality signs relate to the sums of killer cages. Some of the cages have no sum value attached. Digits must not repeat in cages.
Each number at the intersection of cells is the sum of digits in those cells.

Adjacent cells containing consecutive numbers are marked with inequalities. Numbers placed in these cells must satisfy the given inequality. Adjacent cells with no marking must not contain consecutive numbers.
At some intersections of two crossing grid lines, a set of four digits is given. For each set, exactly 2 digits are correct (they appear at least once).
Some cells are divided in half and there must be a digit in any one half.

Two of the gray lines are perfect palindromes and the other two gray lines are consecutive pair palindromes (i.e. paired palindrome positions must have digits which are consecutive to each other). Part of solving is to figure out which lines are perfect palindromes and which are consecutive pair palindromes.

Fill in the grid with digits 1 to 9 so that every row, column and outlined region contains nine different digits. If there are two digits in one cell, the smaller one should be to the left from the higher one.

Some cells are halved by one of the diagonals. For any such cell, exactly one of its two halves shall contain a digit. That digit is considered to belong to the row and column containing its cell, as well as to the region connected to the half cell the digit is in.
Each of the four marked diagonals must also contain each digit from 1-9 exactly once.
Digits do not repeat along any of the three directions in which the hexagonal cells share edges.

The shaded region must be at least once cloned somewhere else in the grid, without rotation or reflection and the digits in the corresponding cells must be equal. The clone may overlap partially with the original shaded region, but not completely. Digits may repeat in a shaded region.

There must be a path from 1 in the arrow to 9 in the target via the digits 2 to 8 so that 2 must be placed in a cell in the direction of the arrow. Consider the cell with 2 a new arrow cell but with the arrow turned 90 degrees to the right. Now 3 must be in a cell in that direction. Consider the cell with 3 a new arrow but with the arrow turned another 90 degrees to the right. Now 4 must be in a cell in that direction ... and so on until you hit the target.
Each digit at the intersection of cells must be present in at least one of those cells.
Each inequality sign (‘<’ or ‘>’) between adjacent cells indicates the larger of the two digits is on the open side of the sign.
For each pair of adjacent boxes, the inequality relationship between three pairs of numbers across the box boundary is the same.
Numbers outside the grid represents sum of 2nd, 3rd and 4th digit in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Place number clues outside the grid.

rules.9x9: Follow classic sudoku rules. Grey cells contain the same digits as the corresponding grey cells in the other puzzle.
Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region.

instructions: Enter a region number in every cell. Cells with the same number form one box; they do not need to be connected. Finish region editing to draw the box borders.

rules.9x9: Some region boundaries are missing, but all given borders must separate cells in different reqions.

Follow irrregular sudoku rules. Rows and columns are marked with numbers 1 to 9. There is a white dot between two cells if the difference of the digits they contain is equal to the number of a row they are part of. There is a black dot between two cells if the sum of the digits they contain is equal to the number of a row they are part of. The same applies to the columns. All possible dots are drawn.
Some regions are connected by lines.

Place numbers from 1-8 in the grid so that each number appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. The numbers on the right and left show the sum of the first 2 numbers in the row from the given side. The numbers on top and bottom show the product of the first 2 numbers in the column from the given side.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each of the empty cells so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined region. Shade some cells in the grid such that the numbers outside the grid represent sums of digits in white cells of the corresponding row or column. If there is more than one sum, the given order is valid and there must be at least one shaded cell between the sums. Some shaded cells may be given.
The number at the top-left corner of each cage is the sum of digits inside the cage. Digits do not repeat within a cage.

instructions: Drag across cells to make a cage, then use Killer Sum on its upper-left corner.

rules.9x9: Fill in the grid with digits 0 to 7 so that every row, column and boldly outlined region contains two zeros and all remaining digits exactly once. Two cells with a zero cannot share an edge. No digit can be repeated in a region with a dashed border. The sum of all digits in every region with a dashed border is given.

Digits places in each marked cage must sum to the total given in its top-left corner. Digits must not repeat in cages. All cages contain a standard arrow clue, which is hidden. The circle part of the arrow will be at one end of the cage with the arrow extending along the cage to the other end. The arrow must fill the entire cage. The circle may enclose any number of digits, and multi-digit totals are read in the direction of the arrow.

The digits placed in each marked cage must sum to the total given in its top-left. Digits must not repeat in cages. Numbers along the arrows indicate the sum of the digits in the corresponding direction.
Two cells which are a knight's move apart must not contain digits which add to either 5 or 15.

Adjacent cells marked with a white circle contain consecutive digits. Adjacent cells marked with a black circle contains digits where one digit is double of the other digit. The circle between 1 and 2 can be of either colour. All possible circles are marked.

instructions: Click an edge to cycle through a black dot, a white dot, and no dot.

rules.9x9: Whenever two adjacent numbers in a row or column add up to 50 or 100, this is marked by a letter L or C respectively, placed on the line separating the two numbers. Two-digit numbers are formed by combining adjacent digits (reading from left to right or top to bottom only). All possible letters L and C are marked. Row and column strings which would result in the leters L and C coinciding (e.g. 5248) will not occur.
Each digit between adjacent cells is the smaller of the digits in those two cells.
Place a letter in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked 3x3 box contains the same set of letters. There are no repeated letters in the set.
Exactly one given clue in each row, column and marked 3 × 3 box is incorrect.

One diagonal must contain each digit from 1-9 exactly once, whilst the other diagonal must have one or more digits repeated. It is part of solving to determine which diagonal has the corresponding property.
The digits placed in all but one marked cage must sum to the total given in its top-left. Exactly one marked cage displays a wrong sum. Digits must not repeat in cages.
Digits in circles must be odd and digits in squares must be even except for exactly one cell which has the wrong symbol.
Each clue in the top-left corner of a marked cage displays two digits correctly and one incorrectly. Digits may repeat in cages.
Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits in the direction pointed by its arrow.

instructions: Number · normal
Shape · diagonal arrow
Place the total and its arrow on the same outside cell.

rules.9x9: There are 80 numbers in the grid that fulfill a following condition: each of these happy numbers has a consecutive friend in a cell sharing an edge with it. There is exactly one lonely number in the grid that doesn't have a consecutive friend next to it.

Three of the given 3x3 blocks are magic squares, which means that the sum of the digits on both diagonals, the three horizontal lines of three cells and the three vertical lines of three cells is the same.

The grey central box serves as an exemplar. The four corner boxes are compared to the central one and the result of comparison is given in the style of a Mastermind puzzle for every triplet of cells. A black dot is given for every identical digit in the same position. A white dot is given for an identical digit but not in the same position. A cross means that there is nothing in common in this triplet.
Some boxes contain four numbers between adjacent cells that are the result of a different mathematical operation (+, -, *, /) of the digits in those cells.

Each number and mathematical operation at the intersection of cells indicates that the number is the result of applying the mathematical operation to each pair of diagonally opposite cells of the intersection. Each 'O' at the intersection of cells indicates that the digits in those cells are odd. Each 'E' at the intersection of cells indicates that the digits in those cells are even. Some numbers may be replaced with '?'.
Also some areas are overlapped, with sizes from 6×6 to 9×9.
Clues outside the grid indicate the length of the longest contiguous run of strictly ascending digits in that row or column viewed from the direction of the clue.

instructions: Number · normal
Enter the ascending sequence length in the margin.
Click an outside cell and type the digit.

rules.9x9: Each number outside the grid is the difference between the highest and the lowest digit in the first three cells in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Number · normal
Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.9x9: In each set of cells connected by meandering diagonal lines each digit appears at most once.
Each number outside the grid is the median of the digits in the first three cells in the corresponding direction.

Place mines into the empty cells in the grid, at most one mine per cell, so that each number in a cell represents the number of mines adjacent to that cell (including diagonally adjacent cells). Each row, column, and outlined region in the grid must contain exactly 3 mines.
Each number outside the grid is the sum of the highest and the lowest digit in the first three cells in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Number · normal
Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.9x9: The four corner boxes contain the same digits in symmetric positions about the centre.
Place a digit from 0-9 in each empty cell. Digits in a row, column and 3x3 box must be different. The clues outside the grid indicate the digit that is not included in the corresponding row/column.

instructions: Place number clues outside the grid.

rules.9x9: The combination of odd and even digits along an arrow corresponds to the "Morse-like coding" for the digit in the circled cell which follows.
No digit can repeat in any of the marked diagonals.
The number in each circle (or oval), reading left-to-right or top-to-bottom, is a multiple or a divisor of the other number it is connected to. The two numbers can be the same.

Some numbers are given on the edges between two adjacent cells. Treating those cells as a two-digit number (reading left to right or top to bottom), the two-digit number must be a multiple of the clue. (For example, near a 7 clue, the two digits can be 14, 21, 28, 35,...)
The number formed from left-to-right in the bottom row of each cage is the product of all the digits in the top row of the cage.

instructions: Cage
Number · edge
Draw each cage; multiplication signs may be placed manually on its internal edges.

rules.9x9: The clues outside the grid indicate the digits that sit next to 9 in that row or column. These clues are given in increasing order.

instructions: Place number clues outside the grid.

rules.9x9: Each line contains each digit from 1 to 9.
For each number 1-9, there must be at least one set of three identical digits in a straight line of adjacent cells somewhere in the grid.

No group of three contiguous cells within any row or column can contain a set of consecutive digits in any order. (For example, the grid cannot have 345, 354, 435, 453, 534, or 543 in three contiguous cells).
Even digits must not be in adjacent cells.
Digits in any three consecutive cells in any row or column must not be of the same parity.
Digits in adjacent cells must not be consecutive.

instructions: This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

rules.9x9: Follow classic sudoku rules. (The classic sudoku regions are marked with the shading not by the bold lines.) Pair of adjacent cells following the spiral way marked by a bold line cannot contain consecutive digits.
Digits must not repeat in cages and their sums end with 5 (the sums can be 5, 15, 25, 35 or 45).
Each digit outside the grid is the digit in the Xth cell in the corresponding direction where X is the digit in the first cell in that direction.

instructions: Number · small
Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.9x9: Cells with shaded circles contain odd digits.
Cells with shaded squares contain even digits. Cells with shaded circles contain odd digits.

instructions: Click a cell center to cycle through an odd circle, an even square, and no mark.

rules.9x9: Follow classic sudoku rules. Sum of the two digits in marked cages is given. Every pair of blank cells (= cells outside marked cages) sharing an edge have different parity.

Some circled cells are connected by a bridge. An odd digit in a circle denotes the number of odd digits on the bridge. An even digit in a circle equals the number of even digits on the bridge. The digits on the circles are not counted. If a circled cell is connected by more than one bridge, an odd/even digit in the cell denote the number of odd/even digits on each connected bridge (not the sum of all connected bridges).

An even digit placed in a cell with a circle indicates the number of even digits placed in the surrounding 8 cells. An odd digit placed in a cell with a circle indicates the number of odd digits placed in the surrounding cells. Numbers placed in cells without a circle may have either of these properties.
Follow classic sudoku rules. Moreover, the sum of two digits in every cage marked with “O” is odd and the sum of two digits in every cage marked with “E” is even.
There must be at least one continuous path of cells containing only odd digits that travels orthogonally from the top-left corner (r1c1) to the bottom-right corner (r9c9).

instructions: No placed input
The constraint is generated from grid geometry.
This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

rules.9x9: Each digit outside the grid indicates that the digit is sandwiched between two odd digits in its adjacent cells in the corresponding direction. All such digits are given.

instructions: Number · normal
Enter every sandwiched digit in one of the three top or left margin layers; leave all three blank to require no sandwiched digit.

rules.9x9: Adjacent cells marked by a circle contain digits whose sum is odd. All possible circles are not marked.

The clues outside the grid indicate, in the correct order, the sums of all consecutive sequences of odd numbers in the corresponding direction. There must be at least one even number placed between two such sequences.
All odd digits must be orthogonally interconnected with no 2x2 area covered completely by odd digits (like shaded cells in Tapa puzzle).
Exactly one odd and one even digit must be placed in each marked pair of adjacent cells.

Place a digit from 1-8 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked 8-cell box contains each number exactly once. The clues outside the grid indicate that the first two cells in the corresponding direction must contain digits matching the following description: O = Odd digits {1, 3, 5, 7}; E = Even digits {2, 4, 6, 8}; B = Big digits {5, 6, 7, 8}; S = Small digits {1, 2, 3, 4}.
A digit in a grey cell must be placed in the Nth position in the next row, where N is the digit immediately to the right of the grey cell.
Some given digits are shaded. All such digits have exactly one digit of the same value one knight step away from its own position.

If a circle is given between two adjacent cells, then the difference in those cells must be 1 or 2. Not all circles are given; adjacent cells without a circle may contain either consecutive digits, digits whose difference is 2 or other non-consecutive digits.

Every digit 1-9 has exactly one touching pair in the grid. All these touches are marked by a circle. If there are less than 9 circles in the grid, that means there is at least one marked 2x2 block which contains two pairs of touching digits.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Digits in the first column say in which column you can find number 1 in the respective row. Digits in the fifth column say in which column you can find number 5. Digits in the nineth column say in which column you can find number 9.
The set of given digits on each grey line must appear on the same line in the solution. They may or may not appear in the same order.
Follow classic sudoku rules. There is a set of 16 different two-digit numbers in the marked cages. Their order from the lowest to the highest is given by small numbers from 1 to 16.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Let’s denote D a number in the marked cell. Let’s denote Aand B the two neighbouring numbers in the cells marked by arrows. The distance between numbers A and B in an ortogonal row / column (marked by the short grey line) should be equal to D. (It is possible to have two signs in one cell.)
Each digit outside the grid appears within the first box in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Number · normal
Enter the three unordered clues in the three margin cells for that direction.

rules.9x9: Follow classic sudoku rules. Numbers outside the grid tells the count of consecutive adjacent pairs in the particular row / column.

instructions: Place number clues outside the grid.

rules.9x9: The signs outside the grid indicate how many inequality signs should be placed between neighbouring cells in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Place number clues outside the grid.

rules.9x9: The clues outside the grid indicate the sum of at least one pair of neighboring cells in that row or column.

instructions: Place number clues outside the grid.

rules.9x9: Each number 'N' outside the grid indicates that the first N digits from the corresponding direction have the same parity and the digit in the 'N+1'th cell has the opposite parity.
Each digit outside the grid appears in one of 2nd, 3rd or 4th cells in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Number · normal
Enter the three unordered clues in the three margin cells for that direction.

rules.9x9: Two grids are overlapping.
Place the digits from 1 to 9 in every row and column of both overlapping grids and in every bold outlined area for the puzzle as a whole.

Shade some cells such that the numbers outside the grid indicate the number of shaded cells in each shaded stretch in the corresponding row or column. If there is more than one number, the given order is valid and there must be at least one white cell between the stretches of shaded cells. Then transfer the digits from the white cells into the blank grid and solve it as classic Sudoku. Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each of the empty cells so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined region.
Digits along each line are a palindrome, they read the same from both directions.

instructions: Drag through cell centers to draw a palindrome line.

rules.9x9: The numbers placed in each shaded region must form the given equations when they are read as the corresponding n-digit numbers.
Digits along any grey line have the same parity (all odd or all even).

Follow classic sudoku rules. If there is an odd number O in a circle, there are exactly O odd numbers around it. If there is an even number E in a circle, there are exactly E even numbers around it. All possible circles are given.
Digits along each line are either all odd or all even.
Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits up to and including the first even digit or up to and including the first odd digit from the corresponding direction.

Each digit represents the height of a building. The clues outside the grid indicate whether an odd (O) or even (E) number of buildings is visible from the corresponding direction. A taller building will hide any shorter buildings behind it.

instructions: Place number clues outside the grid.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some cells belong to multiple rows and/or columns.

Clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the digit(s) in the respective row or column, when the row or column is partitioned at certain points (not given), in the correct order. Clues are either given for the entire row or column, or not given at all.
Cells with pencilmark digits must contain one of those digits.

instructions: Number · candidates
Toggle every allowed digit in the marked cell.

rules.9x9: Adjacent cells, reading left-to-right or top-to-bottom, that represent a two-digit perfect square are marked in the grid. All such perfect squares are marked. The list of perfect squares is 16, 25, 36, 49, 64 and 81.
Exactly one of the highlighted digits is a pinnochio and is incorrect.

instructions: Number · normal · style 0
Enter every highlighted candidate digit directly in its cell.

rules.9x9: Place the given fragments inside the grid without rotating or mirroring. Fragments can touch each other, but can not overlap each other or given digits.

Follow irregular sudoku rules (digits 1 to 7). No digit can be repeated in a region with a dashed border. The sum of all digits in every region with a dashed border is given while digits in grey cells are counted as negative ones.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Cell with a horizontal line contains a sum of the two neighbouring cells in the particular row. Cell with a vertical line contains a difference of the two neighbouring cells in the particular column. All possible lines are given.

A grey horizontal or vertical bar symbol in a cell indicates that the digit in the cell is equal to the sum or difference of its horizontal or vertical neighbours. Not all possible bars are marked; cells without a bar may or may not indicate either sum or difference of its horizontal or vertical neighbours.
If digit N is placed in a cell with an arrow, digit N+1 must be placed in one of the cells pointed by the arrow.
If digit N is placed in a cell with an arrow, digit N-1 must be placed in one of the cells pointed by the arrow.
Numbers outside the grid indicate the number of different numbers placed in the cells in the corresponding diagonal direction.

instructions: Place diagonal arrow symbols outside the grid, and enter the count digits.

rules.9x9: A digit N in a cell with an arrow sees another digit N, which is N cells away in the arrow's direction (diagonally). Arrows may be reciprocal (pointing towards each other). Not all possible arrows are given.
Numbers outside the grid signify the position of the highest of the nearest three digits in the corresponding row/column.

Apply classic sudoku rule. External clues give information relating to the first two digits (called A and B) in the corresponding row or column. Clues next to the grid give the sum of A and B. Clues in the grey band further outside the grid give the sum of the digits in positions A and B.
Place digits from 1 to 9, they don't repeat in marked triangles and rows of cells in three principal directions (of any length, e.g.4+5).
Horizontally and vertically adjacent cells containing digits whose sum is a prime number are marked with a diamond. All possible diamonds are marked.
Each digit between adjacent cells is the product of digits in those two cells.
Some arrows are in the grid. The encircled 1, 2, or more digits must be equal to the product of the digits that appear along the path of the arrow. Digits can repeat within an arrow shape.
Each number outside the grid is the product of the first three digits in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Number · M
Arrow · Eight directions
Enter each product in the outside margin and place an arrow in the same cell to set its direction.

rules.9x9: The number at the top-left corner of each cage is the product of digits inside the cage. Digits do not repeat within a cage.

instructions: Cage
Number · Killer
Draw each cage, then enter its product in the cage corner.

rules.9x9: Follow classic sudoku rules. Number in a circle is equal to the last digit of a product of all digits along the corresponding arrow.
Each number outside the grid is the product of digits in the direction pointed by its arrow.

instructions: Number · M
Shape · diagonal arrow
Place the product and its arrow on the same outside cell.

rules.9x9: There are some pyramids in the grid made out of grey cells. For each row above the base of the pyramid, each grey cell must equal either the sum or the difference of the two grey cells diagonally beneath it.
The digit pointed by each arrow must be larger than the other three digits that the arrow touches.

instructions: Shape · diagonal arrow

rules.9x9: The digit pointed by each arrow must be smaller than the other three digits that the arrow touches.

instructions: Shape · diagonal arrow

rules.9x9: A circle at a corner implies that one digit is the sum of the remaining three digits at that 2x2 square. Not all possible dots are given, i.e. there may or may not be additional 2x2 squares where one digit is the sum of the remaining three digits.
No 2x2 area contains four digits of the same parity.
The four digits at the intersection of four cells must be present in those four cells.

instructions: Number · corner · circle background

rules.9x9: The digit 9 must not be repeated in the same diagonal of any length.
A digit 'X' in a circle means that the digit in the cell is the X-th smallest number in the corresponding cage. Digits cannot repeat within a cage.
The digits placed in adjacent cells must satisfy the given ratios.

Within each 3x3 region, exactly one of two constraints (A or B) applies: (A) Digits places in adjacent cells within the 3x3 region must not be consecutive, (B) Each cell must be adjacent to at least one other cell within the 3x3 region where the digits places in both are consecutive. Neither constraint applies to adjacent cells in different 3x3 regions.

For each line, digits on the line have an equal sum N within each box it passes through. If a line passes through the same box more than once, each individual segment of such a line within that box sums to N separately.
Lines in a cell mark that in those two directions both digits are the same at a distance of N where N is the value of that cell. Not all possible lines are given.
Each marked line contains a set of consecutive digits. Digits do not repeat within a line.

instructions: Draw lines through cells.

rules.9x9: Each marked region contains a set of consecutive digits. Digits do not repeat within a region.

instructions: Draw cages around cells.

rules.9x9: Each outlined cage must contain a set of distinct digits with the specified digit sum. All remaining squares form contiguous blocks of shaded cells and each block must contain a set of consecutive digits.
All cells having the same digit(s) more than once as orthogonal neighbors are shaded. (If a cell is not shaded, it cannot have any digits repeated as orthogonal neighbors.)

Instead of clues for the digits 1 to 9, this sudoku gives clues for Roman numerals I, II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, IX. Not all clues are complete numbers; some of them may be just part of the Roman numeral.
Each arrow outside the grid indicates that the digits in the first three cells in the corresponding direction are in ascending order in the direction of the arrow. All such arrows are marked.

instructions: Shape · thin black arrow
Place cardinal arrows in the margin. Diagonal arrows are unavailable.

rules.9x9: Each cage clue indicates the value of rounding off the two-digit number within that cage to the nearest multiple of 10. Numbers ending in digits 1-4 are rounded down, while numbers ending in digits 5-9 are rounded up.
The sum of four cells adjacent to a grey one is the same in the whole grid.
Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits between 1 and 9 in the corresponding row or column.

instructions: Click an outside clue cell and type the sandwich sum.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column, outlined region and the set of shaded cells.

instructions: Enter a region number in every cell, then shade one grid-size set of cells. The shading identifies the Scattered set visually and is not a generic shading rule.

rules.9x9: Place the digits from 1 to 9 in every row, column, bold outlined area and the nine grey cells. Clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the first X digits in the corresponding direction. X is the first digit in the corresponding direction.
Each arrow points to at least one 9 in the corresponding direction. Each digit in a cell with an arrow is the distance from that cell to the nearest cell containing a 9.

Some arrows are marked in the grid. Each arrow points to the digit 9 in the respective row, column or diagonal. The digit in the cell with the arrow is the distance from this cell to the corresponding digit 9. For each diagonally oriented arrow, the digit in the cell with the arrow shows the distance to the closest digit 9 in that direction.
Some arrows are marked in the grid. Each arrow points to the digit 9 in the respective row or column. The digit in the cell with the arrow is the distance from this cell to the corresponding digit 9.
Digits along each line are in arithmetic progression.
The digits along each marked line have to be different and must form an arithmetic sequence in the correct order. Two straight lines may cross each other.

Each number outside the rows is the sum of digits in the 2nd and 3rd cells in the corresponding direction. Each number outside the columns is the sum of digits in the 3rd and 4th cells in the corresponding direction.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 in each empty cell so that each digit appears exactly once in every row and column. In bold regions of size N ≤ 9, the digits 1 to N must appear exactly once. In bold regions of size N > 9, the (N-9) largest digits will appear twice while the remaining digits will appear once. For example, a region with 11 cells will contain the digits 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 8, 9, 9, with the two largest digits, 8 and 9, repeated.

Blacken exactly two cells in every row, column and outlined region. Fill in the remaining blank cells with digits from a given set so that no digit is repeated in a row, column or outlined region. The sum of all digits between the two black cells is given for some rows and columns.
Each shape is cloned, including the digits it contains. Position of digits within the shape may change. A cloned shape may undergo rotation and/or reflection. Digits may repeat in a shape.
Each shaded shape below the grid must replace one of the shaded regions in the grid. Shapes can be rotated, but cannot be reflected.

Each digit represents the height of a building. The clues outside the grid indicate either the number of even-heighted buildings or number of odd-heighted buildings visible from the corresponding direction, or both. A taller building will hide any shorter buildings behind it.

Each digit inside the grid represents the height of a skyscraper in that cell. Each number outside the grid indicates the number of skyscrapers that can be seen in the corresponding row or column. Taller skyscrapers hide shorter ones.

instructions: Click an outside clue cell and type its visibility count.

rules.9x9: Follow classic sudoku rules. Digits along the arrows represent buildings of the height given by its value. Number in a circle tells you the count of visible buildings along the corresponding arrow, in the direction from the circle to the opposite end of the arrow. Higher building covers all smaller or equal building behind it.
In each row the cells between two poles must contain a set of consecutive digits.
The digits in each marked row or column are in exactly the same cyclic sequence wrapped around the grid.

instructions: Input-mode column checkboxes
Toggle columns in the Input Modes section; the editor paints each selected column.

rules.9x9: One or more loops of orthogonally connected cells will be shaded grey. Along such a loop, each pair of adjacent digits must have a difference of either 1 or 2.
Each arrow(s) in a cell points to the smallest digit among the digits in its adjacent cells.

instructions: Shape · eight-direction arrow
Select every arrow pointing to a smallest neighbour.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1-6 in each empty cell so that each digit appears exactly once in each “row“ and outlined hexagon.
The sum of digits in every cage is the same throughout the grid. It is part of solving to determine the sum.

instructions: Cage
Draw every equal-sum cage; no cage total is entered.

rules.9x9: In each cage, at least one cell has the indicated digit.

instructions: Drag across cells to make a cage, then use Killer Sum on its upper-left corner.

rules.9x9: Exactly three of the nine 3x3 boxes have the property that their eight perimeter cells are in a strictly increasing order with starting cell and direction unspecified. The 3 sprial boxes, and the starting cell/direction in these boxes need to be identified as part of solving.
The digits inside each square wheel must be located in those four cells in the given order. Square wheels can be rotated, but not reflected.
The clues outside the grid indicate the product of the digits placed in the cells containing stars in the corresponding row or column.

Follow classic sudoku rules. There is exactly one way how to go in a cycle through all grey arrows, every time making that number of steps to the next arrow which is given by the digit in the previous cell with an arrow
Digits along each thermometer are non-decreasing from its bulb to each of its ends.

instructions: Special · thermometer
Draw each branched or straight non-decreasing thermometer from its bulb.

rules.9x9: For every pair of connected cages, the set of digits in the smaller cage must be a subset of the set of digits in the larger cage. Digits on the corresponding connecting line must belong to the set of digits in the larger cage but must not belong to the subset of digits in the smaller cage. Digits are not allowed to repeat in any cage.
A cell with an alphabet contains a digit whose mapped word contains the corresponding alphabet.
Some rows and columns are bent, marked by curved lines.

If any operation (+, -, x, /) performed between two orthogonally adjacent cells leads to a result of N, then the two cells are separated by a grey circle. If there is no grey circle, the result can not be N. The number N can be any integer in the range from 1-9. N is not given. It is part of solving to determine N.

Place either a digit from 1 to 7 or a star in each empty cell, so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. There must be exactly two stars in in each row, column and outlined region. Stars cannot be placed in adjacent cells that touch even at a corner.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each of the empty cells so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and irregular shaped outlined region. The sum of digits in each horizontal and vertical group of cells is given on its left and top respectively.
Each digit between adjacent cells is the sum of digits in those two cells.
The sum of the numbers placed in corresponding cells in two regions of the same shape must equal 10.
The sum of numbers in the shaded cells in each row is exactly 100. Two adjacent shaded cells in the same row form a two-digit number.

There are 22 outlined cages in the grid that have sums between 5 and 15. Every sum is used exactly twice and the combinations of digits never repeat. Some of the sums are already given. Digits may repeat in a cage.
Each number outside the grid is the sum of the first few digits from the edge. The nearest shaded cell in the corresponding direction indicates the number of digits included in the sum.
Each arrow in the grid indicates that the sum of the first N digits along the direction pointed by the arrow is equal to the digit in the cell for some value of N.

instructions: Shape · eight-direction arrow
At each origin, toggle every direction containing the required prefix sum.

rules.9x9: Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits within the first box in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Number · small
Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.9x9: Follow diagonal sudoku rules. Each row and both diagonals can be interpreted as a sequence of three numbers, a 4-digit, 3-digit (in grey cells) and 2-digit one. The total of those numbers is given.
Clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the immediate neighbours of the digit 9 in the corresponding row or column.
Adjacent cells containing numbers with the sum 9 are marked with a rhombus. Adjacent cells with no marking must not contain numbers with the sum 9.
The number in each cage shows either the sum or the product (or both) of all digits inside the cage. Digits may be repeated within a cage.

The clues outside the grid indicate all numbers placed in the corresponding direction that are the sum of the numbers placed in the two neighbouring cells in that direction, e.g. 132. If a number is not clued outside the grid, then it must not be the sum of its two neighbours in that direction.
The sum of the digits in each marked cage must be different.

Each number represents the height of a building. The clues outside the grid indicate the sum of buildings visible from the corresponding direction. A taller building will hide any shorter buildings behind it.

Follow classic sudoku rules. There is one circled number N in every box. If any number of that particular box has a neighbour sharing an edge which is differing exactly by N there is a dot between them. (All possible dots are marked.)

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. Each outlined region of more than one cell contains digits from 1 to 9 at least once.

instructions: Enter a region number in every cell. Equal numbers form an outlined region; Surplus regions may contain more than the grid-size number of cells.

rules.9x9: The contents of any two cells in symmetrical positions relative to the centre of the grid must sum to 10. Digits placed in cells connected by a chess Knight's move must be different.

Each rectangular region is of size 2xn and the number in the circle is the sum of the products of all 180-degree symmetric pairs of digits within the region. Digits may be repeated within a rectangular region.
Cells that are 180° symmetric about the centre must not contain the same digit.

Apply class sudoku rules. Within cages, digits must increase from top to bottom along columns and increase along rows from left to right, even if there is a gap between two cells in a cage. Digits within a cage must be distinct.
If a gray bar is given between two adjacent cells, then the two digits in those cells sum to ten or eleven. If a gray bar is not given, the two digits cannot sum to ten or eleven.

Number between a pair of adjacent cell indicates the number of the tens position of the product of the numbers placed in these cells (e.g. if 3 and 4 are placed in the cells, their product is 12 and the clue would be 1).
Digits along each thermometer are strictly increasing from its bulb to each of its ends.

instructions: Drag from the bulb through the cells in increasing order.

rules.9x9: In each group of three lines: - One will follow Arrow Sudoku rules. The digit placed in a cell with a circle must be the sum of the digits placed in the cells that the adjoining arrow passes through. Digits may repeat on arrows. It is up to the solver to determine which end has the circle. - One will follow Thermo Sudoku rules. Starting at the “bulb”, digits placed along the thermometer must form a strictly increasing sequence. It is up to the solver to determine which end has the bulb. - One will follow Palindrome Sudoku rules. Digits along the line read the same in both directions. It is allowed that a line has properties of both Arrow and Thermo Sudoku (for instance: 1-2-3-6).
Digits in the gray strings represent three-digit numbers. All three-digit numbers in the cage must sum to the total given in its top-left corner. Digits must not repeat in cages.

The central 3x3 region serves as a map to the 9 regions of the grid (for the example, its top-left cell corresponds to the top-left 3x3 region, and its central cell corresponds to the central 3x3 region). Wherever the central region contains an odd digit, the corresponding region must have three odd digits in at least one line (horizontal, vertical or diagonal). Wherever it contains an even digit, the corresponding region must have three even digits in a line. No region can contain both a line of odd digits and a line of even digits.

Each 3x3 box is also a grid for a Tic-Tac-Toe game. A game is won by placing three odd digits or three even digits in a horizontal, vertical or diagonal line. Each 3x3 box contains exactly one winning combination and this combination is marked by a grey line.

Place a digit (or two digits in split cells) from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. In each cell split into halves, the smaller digit must be above the larger digit.
Each marked line contains exactly one identical digit.

instructions: Line · diagonal · grey
Draw each marked line with the grey diagonal style.

rules.9x9: For every pair of vertically adjacent digits where both digits lie in the range 1-7, the upper digit must be greater than the lower digit.
Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some outlined regions wrap around the grid.

Complete the grid so that each row, column and 3x3 box contains 8 distinct digits in the range 1-9. The digits in the cells orthogonally adjacent to each black square must give an identical total sum.
Each digit has at least one consecutive digit in its adjacent cells.

The digits in the gray cells have been erased from a filled Classic Sudoku grid. Then four digits in every row are transferred to the right one after another. Also, four digits in every column are transferred down one after another. Each digit that was not erased was moved outside the grid to either a row or column clue, but not both. Restore all digits to the grid to reform the starting Sudoku.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined region. Cells with circles contain digits 1, 2 or 3. Cells with squares contain digits 4, 5 or 6.
rules.9x9: Cells with circles contain digits 1, 2 or 3. Cells with squares contain digits 4, 5 or 6. Cells with triangles contain digits 7, 8 or 9.

Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell or blacken the cell so that each digit appears exactly once in every row, column, and outlined 3x3 region along with three black cells. Each black cell should be part of an orthogonally connected group of three blackened cells (a triomino). No two triominoes can share an edge.

Each row is divided into 3 parts. The 1st part is a 4-digit number, the 2nd part (shaded) is a 3-digit number and the 3rd part is a 2-digit number. The sum of these three numbers is given outside the grid in the corresponding row. Not all outside clues may be given.
Wherever the number in the cell equals to a total of any amount of the closest numbers in any direction, there is an arrow pointing to that direction possible arrows are given).
Adjacent cells in the indicated direction (left-to-right or top-to-bottom) containing a two-digit prime number are marked with a diamond. All possible diamonds are marked.
Chess Knights, placed in cells with number 9, must attack only different numbers.

variants.otherNames: Ordered sums Sudoku, Unique Order Sums Sudoku
variants.name: Unique Ordered Sums

rules.9x9: There are numbered cages in the grid. All cages are ordered according to the sum of digits in the cage. A cage marked with number 1 has the lowest sum, a cage with number 2 has a higher sum than a cage marked with number 1 … and so on. It is possible for two or more cages marked with the same number to appear in the grid. That means they have the same sum (not necessary the same digits).
Follow classic sudoku rules. If any four cells in the grid lays in exactly two rows and two columns, there should be more than two different digits in them.
Shaded regions must contain a set of distinct, consecutive digits in any order. Every renban group consists of an unique set of digits.
The digits along a grey line goes alternately up and down while the difference between the two neighbouring digits is at least 4.

A digit N in a grey cell represents a watchtower, that can overlook exactly N cells, including itself, with digits smaller than N in all four directions up to the first larger digit in that direction. All possible watchtowers are given.

The clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the digits contained in the cells in the direction of the corresponding arrow. Digits in gray cells have to be counted twice. Digits may repeat within a sum.
The digits inside each wheel must be located in those four cells in the given order. Wheels can be rotated, but not reflected.

instructions: Number · Tapa
Shape · large circle
Place four clue digits and a large circle at the same four-cell intersection.

rules.9x9: The digits on the circles have to be placed in the same circular order in the four cells that are touched by the circle. The circles may have to be rotated to the correct position by 90, 180 or 270 degrees.
One wildcard. Cells with '>' contain digits greater than wildcard. Cells with '<' contain digits smaller than wildcard.
Each marked 3x3 region contains each digit from 1 to 9.

instructions: Cage
On a 9 × 9 grid, four 3 × 3 cages are created automatically at r2c2, r2c5, r5c2, and r5c5.

rules.9x9: Place a symbol from the given set into each of the empty cells so that each symbol appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined region. Each given word must appear horizontally, vertically, or diagonally - forward or backward, in the grid.
The clues outside the grid differ by exactly 1 from the sum of the first 3 numbers placed in the corresponding direction.
Clues outside the grid indicate the exact average of the first X digits seen from that side, where X is the first digit from that side.
The number placed in a circled cell must appear exactly once on each of the two marked diagonals passing through that cell.
Each number outside the grid is the sum of the first X numbers placed in the corresponding direction, where X is the first digit placed in that direction.

instructions: Number · small
Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.9x9: Adjacent cells with digits summing to 6 are marked by VI. Adjacent cells with digits summing to 11 are marked by XI. All possible VI and XI are marked.

instructions: Number · edge
Place VI on every adjacent pair summing to 6 and XI on every pair summing to 11.

rules.9x9: Adjacent cells with digits summing to 5 are marked by V. Adjacent cells with digits summing to 10 are marked by X. All possible V and X are marked.

instructions: Click an edge to cycle through V, X, and no clue.

rules.9x9: Horizontally adjacent cells marked by a circle (or diamond) contain digits whose absolute difference is equal to the first digit of the corresponding row. Vertically adjacent cells marked by a circle (or diamond) contain digits whose absolute difference is equal to the first digit of the corresponding column. All such circles (or diamonds) are marked.
Digits in the top-left corner of a marked cage must all appear in the cage. Digits may repeat in cages.

instructions: Drag across cells to make a cage, then use Killer Sum on its upper-left corner.

rules.9x9: Equal digits may not be a chess king move apart.

instructions: This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

rules.9x9: Outside clues indicate the sum of the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th cells in that row or column in that direction.
Outside clues indicate the digit that is missing from the first three cells in that row or column in that direction.
Outside clues indicate the sum of the immediate neighbors of the digit 9 in that row or column.
Outside clues indicate the count of consecutive adjacent pairs in that row or column.
Outside clues indicate the count of neighboring cells in that row or column that contain a digit greater than their neighbor in that direction.
Outside clues indicate the sum of at least one pair of neighboring cells in that row or column.
Outside clues indicate whether an odd or even number of skyscrapers are visible in that direction.
Outside clues indicate the count of distinct digits along the diagonal starting in that gonalrection.
